coredata.dc:title: Improving the mechanical property of silk by feeding silkworm with chiral carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yan
Zhang, Mengling
Li, Wenwen
Hu, Tao
Liu, Yang
Huang, Hui
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: The influence of chiral materials on organisms is crucial. However, there is little research on the impact of chiral carbon dots (CDs), a kind of typical chiral materials, on biology. Herein, chiral CDs (L-/D-CDs) were synthesized using the thermal polymerization method from citric acid and chiral cysteine. The effect of chiral CDs on silkworms was explored through feeding silkworms with chiral CDs. The breaking strength of silk fibers (667.9 MPa) in D-CDs group exhibit a 71.4 % increase compared with control-silk (389.5 MPa), while the breaking strength of silk fibers in L-CDs group increases by 51.6 %. In addition, Fourier transform infrared spectra display CDs can prevent the transformation from random coil/α-helix structures to β-sheet structures. Furthermore, D-CDs group exhibit the highest percentage of four primary amino acids (glycine, alanine, serine, and tyrosine) relative to the total amino acids in silkworm hemolymph. This percentage is elevated by 70.5 % compared to the control group, thereby furnishing an ample supply of raw materials for the synthesis of silk proteins. In contrast, L-CDs group exhibit increase by 39.3 %. Our work provides new ideas and approaches for studying the effects of chiral materials on living organisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon dots
Silkworm silk
Mechanical property
Chiral biological effects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of chiral CDs and achiral CDs
Preparation of CDs modified diets and silkworm raising
Measurements of amino acid content and enzymatic activity in vivo
Effects of chiral CDs on the growth and cocoon formation of silkworm
Mechanical properties and structural characterization of silk fibers
Effects of chiral CDs on silkworm in vivo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BE2022425, BK20230503), National Natural Science Foundation of China (52271223, 52272043, 52302107), Key R & D program of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (2022BEG02006), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of D-CDs. (a) TEM image and size distribution of D-CDs. (b) UV–Vis absorption and ECD spectra of L-CDs and D-CDs. (c) PL emission (red line) and PL excitation (grey line) spectra of D-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of D-Cys and D-CDs. (e) Full-scan XPS survey spectra of D-CDs. (f) High-resolution XPS C 1s spectra of D-CDs.

Effects of chiral CDs (1.0 mg/mL) on the growth and cocoon formation of silkworm. (a) The schematic diagram of the acquired process for silkworms by feeding with L-CDs and D-CDs. (b) Body weights of silkworms of control group and CDs groups (L-CDs, D-CDs, and AC-CDs), (c-f) Photographs of the obtained cocoons, (g-j) SEM images of silk fibers, (k) Average weights of the obtained cocoon shells, (l) Average diameter of the obtained cocoons, and (m) Average diameter of silk fibers. Data are presented as the mean values ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis is evaluated by comparing with control group (ns represents no statistical difference, P < 0.05 is indicated by a single asterisk (*), P < 0.01 is indicated by a double asterisk (**), and P < 0.001 is indicated by a triple asterisk (***)).

Influence of chiral CDs feeding on the mechanical properties and secondary structures of silk fibers. (a) Stress–strain curves of silk fibers of control group and CDs groups (1.0 mg/mL). (b) Relative stress and strain of silk fibers from silkworm fed with different concentrations of CDs (compare with control group). (c) FT-IR spectra, (d) random coil/α-helix content based on FT-IR results, and (e) Raman spectra of silk fibers for the control group and CDs groups (1.0 mg/mL). (f) Schematic diagram for the reinforcing mechanism of silk fibers. Data are presented as the mean values ± SD. Statistical analysis is evaluated by comparing with control group (ns represents no statistical difference, P < 0.05 is indicated by a single asterisk (*), P < 0.01 is indicated by a double asterisk (**), and P < 0.001 is indicated by a triple asterisk (***)).

Effects of chiral CDs on silkworm in vivo. (a) The percentage of four main amino acids (serine, tyrosine, glycine and alanine) to the total amino acids in the silkworm hemolymph. (b) The content of main amino acids in the silkworm hemolymph. (c) Alkaline phosphatase activity and (d) reduced glutathione content in the midgut tissue of silkworms. For the CDs group, the CDs concentration is 1.0 mg/mL. Data are presented as the mean values ± SD. Statistical analysis is evaluated by comparing with control group (ns represents no statistical difference, P < 0.05 is indicated by a single asterisk (*), P < 0.01 is indicated by a double asterisk (**), and P < 0.001 is indicated by a triple asterisk (***)).

The absorption and metabolism of chiral CDs in silkworm. CLSM images of (a) silk fibers and (b) silkworm excrement (measured with an excitation wavelength of 382 nm). Fluorescent intensity of (c) silk fibers and (d) silkworm excrement. For the CDs group, the CDs concentration is 1.0 mg/mL. Data are presented as the mean values ± SD. Statistical analysis is evaluated by comparing with control group (ns represents no statistical difference, P < 0.05 is indicated by a single asterisk (*), P < 0.01 is indicated by a double asterisk (**), and P < 0.001 is indicated by a triple asterisk (***)).

head.ce:title: Improving the mechanical property of silk by feeding silkworm with chiral carbon dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The influence of chiral materials on organisms is crucial. However, there is little research on the impact of chiral carbon dots (CDs), a kind of typical chiral materials, on biology. Herein, chiral CDs (L-/D-CDs) were synthesized using the thermal polymerization method from citric acid and chiral cysteine. The effect of chiral CDs on silkworms was explored through feeding silkworms with chiral CDs. The breaking strength of silk fibers (667.9 MPa) in D-CDs group exhibit a 71.4 % increase compared with control-silk (389.5 MPa), while the breaking strength of silk fibers in L-CDs group increases by 51.6 %. In addition, Fourier transform infrared spectra display CDs can prevent the transformation from random coil/α-helix structures to β-sheet structures. Furthermore, D-CDs group exhibit the highest percentage of four primary amino acids (glycine, alanine, serine, and tyrosine) relative to the total amino acids in silkworm hemolymph. This percentage is elevated by 70.5 % compared to the control group, thereby furnishing an ample supply of raw materials for the synthesis of silk proteins. In contrast, L-CDs group exhibit increase by 39.3 %. Our work provides new ideas and approaches for studying the effects of chiral materials on living organisms.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality plays a crucial role in organisms as it determines the structure and function of biological molecules [1]. The two different enantiomers of chiral molecules may have completely different bioactivities within organisms. Interestingly, DNA and RNA exist only in the form of right-handedness in nature, while proteins exhibit left-handed characteristics [2–5]. This preference for a single chirality may be the result of evolutionary processes, related to the origin of life and the optimization of the functions of biological molecules. Moreover, organisms typically have a high degree of chiral selectivity for the molecules that construct their structures and perform functions. In recent years, extensive research on chiral nanomaterials has penetrated into fields such as drug design, biomedicine, and biosensing [6–10]. However, the research on the relationship between chirality and biological response is still in its early stages. The impact of chiral nanomaterials on living organisms requires in-depth exploration.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a typical carbon-based nanomaterial, have the advantages of tunable fluorescence, low toxicity or even non-toxicity, good biocompatibility, and low cost, which possess the properties of both a semiconductor (at the core) and an organic molecule (on the surface) [11–16]. After CDs are endowed with chirality, they will exhibit more diverse properties and broad applications. Chiral CDs not only combine the basic advantages of CDs, but also have unique chiral characteristics. They are widely used in bioimaging, drug delivery, biomarkers, and chiral catalysis and so on [17–20]. For instance, chiral CDs show the selective capacity for electrocatalytic oxidization of tryptophan enantiomers [20]. In addition, chiral CDs display the enantioselective catalytic activity on DOPA enantiomers oxidation [21]. However, as a typical chiral material, there is relatively little research on the impact of chiral CDs on biology.

Silkworms are a widely recognized model organism, with a long history and significant status in biological research [22–25]. They have a short life cycle and reproduce rapidly. The lower cost of raising silkworms makes it possible to conduct large-scale experiments. Therefore, using chiral CDs to feed silkworms is one of the best choices for exploring the effects of chiral CDs on living organisms. Silk has been widely explored and developed for textiles, the preparation of biomedical materials, optoelectronic devices, flexible electronic materials, and biosensors. As previously reported by Liu et al. [26], fluorescent CDs made from mulberry leaves, which have a strong near-infrared fluorescence, can be potentially used as silkworm diets and silkworms exhibit bright red fluorescence and grow healthily. Therefore, research studies on the effects of chiral CDs on silkworms are crucial.

Herein, chiral CDs (L-/D-CDs) were synthesized using the thermal polymerization method with citric acid (CA) and cysteine. We aim to evaluate the chiral biological effects of these CDs on the properties and structure of silk fibers. Compared with the control group without the intake of CDs, silks feeding with CDs show significantly enhanced mechanical properties, including superior elongation at break and breaking strength. Analysis of the silk structure using Raman and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) revealed that D-CDs impeded the structural transition from random coil/α-helix to β-sheet, resulting in enhanced extensibility and strength. Besides, further analysis of enzyme activities and amino acid contents provides additional insights into the biological pathways affected by chiral CDs intake. Through an examination of the properties and structure of silk fibers, it is revealed that the intake of CDs exerts chiral biological influences on silk fibers, thereby paving the way for optimizing methods of producing tailored silk fibers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of chiral CDs and achiral CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Chiral CDs were prepared by the thermal polymerization method with chiral cysteine and CA as raw materials. Firstly, 3 g CA was heated in an oil bath at 200 °C for 30 min to acquire the carbon core. Then, 3 g L-/D-Cysteine (L-/D-Cys) was added and thoroughly mixed, cooled down to 150 °C and kept for 60 min. Subsequently, the crude product was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, followed by filtration to remove large particles and unreacted precursors. After that, the purified chiral CDs (L-/D-CDs) were obtained using dialysis bag (500–1000 Da) for 3 days. A similar procedure was adopted to prepare the achiral CDs (AC-CDs) using CA as the precursor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs modified diets and silkworm raising
ce:section.ce:para: Bombyx mori were raised in dedicated silkworm rearing rooms from egg to fifth instar stage. During this time, the larvae were exclusively provided with fresh mulberry leaves as food until the second day of the fifth instar stage [27].

Next, L-/D-CDs and AC-CDs at concentrations of 0.5 mg/mL and 1.0 mg/mL were obtained and dispensed into spray cans for administration. Subsequently, the dispersed CDs suspension was evenly sprayed on the surface of fresh mulberry leaves, which were subsequently air-dried. While the exact volume of solution applied each time was not meticulously regulated, a consistent total of 100 mL CDs suspension was administered to each group of silkworms before cocooning. Besides, the control group was fed a normal diet of fresh mulberry leaves for comparison.

Subsequently, all silkworms were evenly divided into seven groups, namely the control group, L-CDs group, D-CDs group, and AC-CDs group. From the second day of the fifth instar stage onwards, silkworms were raised until cocooning with the control group consuming normal diet and the CDs groups fed mulberry leaves containing the respective CDs.

The obtained cocoons were first subjected to drying at 100 °C for 60 min before the degumming process. Next, a certain amount of cocoon shells were boiled in a 0.5 wt% Na2CO3 aqueous solution at 100 °C. During the degumming process, continuous stirring with a glass rod was required to ensure uniform silk degumming. After 30 min, cocoon shells were rinsed with deionized water at 50–60 °C, and the above degumming process was repeated three times. Finally, the obtained degummed silk fibers were dried at 60 °C for 12 h [28].

A material testing instrument (Instron 3365, America) was used for measuring the mechanical properties of degummed silk fibers. The tensile rate is 2 mm/min and the gauge length of silk fibers is 20 mm. Before testing, the tested silk were balanced in a constant temperature and humidity (25 °C, 65 %) laboratory for 24 h. Assuming the cross-section of the silk fiber is circular, the load strain curve is transformed into a stress-strain curve to obtain tensile strength and elongation at break. For each sample, >20 single silk fibers were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of amino acid content and enzymatic activity in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments of amino acid contents in the hemolymph were conducted by amino acid analysis. Briefly, 5 mL hemolymph was dispersed in 5 mL sulfosalicylic acid (5 %) evenly, followed by centrifugation. The resulting supernatant was dried by evaporation, dissolved in 1 mL of sodium citrate buffer, and filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane. After pre-treatment to remove protein impurities, 1 mL of the sample was taken and amino acid composition and contents were analyzed using an amino acid analyzer (Biochrom, Britain). The enzymatic activity in midgut was estimated with alkaline phosphatase (ALP) kit and reducing glutathione (GSH) kit according to the manufacturer's instructions. 0.1 g midgut tissue was homogenized in 1 mL extraction solution on ice. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 at 4 °C, the supernatant was collected and kept on ice for analysis. The determination of ALP activity and GSH activity were measured at 405 nm and 412 nm, respectively. Each sample was tested in triplicate across three independent experiments.

Chiral CDs (L-/D-CDs) were obtained by modifying chiral Cys (L-Cys or D-Cys) on achiral carbonated CA core. Detailed synthesis process is shown in the experimental section. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used for investigating the micromorphology of chiral CDs. Which reveals that D-CDs exhibit a nearly spherical morphology with an average size of 3.2 nm (Fig. 1a), while L-CDs appear spheroid-like with an average size of 3.8 nm (Fig. S1). UV–Vis absorption and electronic circular dichroism (ECD) spectra were utilized to characterize the optical properties and chirality of CDs. UV–Vis absorbance spectra of L-/D-CDs show a similar absorption (Fig. 1b), showing two absorption bands at 247 nm and 351 nm. The former band corresponds to π−π* conjugation of the sp2 hybridized carbon network, while the latter band is attributed to n−π* transitions due to the presence of surface functional groups such as carboxyl amino moieties [29,30]. In Fig. 1b, UV–Vis spectrum of D-CDs (red line) exhibits a negative ECD signal at 245 nm and 353 nm, whereas L-CDs (black line) display opposite ECD signals. Additionally, both L-/D-CDs demonstrate photoluminescence (PL) properties. As illustrated in Fig. 1c, D-CDs reveal an optimal emission peak at 426 nm with the excitation wavelength of 331 nm, while L-CDs reveal an optimal emission peak at 429 nm with the excitation wavelength of 336 nm (Fig. S2).

FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were employed for analyzing the composition and structure of chiral CDs. FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1d) display the surface functional groups of D-Cys and D-CDs, with characteristic peaks at 3467 cm−1 for O−H stretching [31], 1635 cm−1 for CO stretching and 1375 cm−1 for C−N stretching vibrations [32]. Notably, D-CDs present an additional peak at 1215 cm−1 corresponding to C−S bonds, not observed in the raw material (D-Cys) FT-IR spectrum [33]. Conversely, L-Cys/L-CDs exhibit similar absorption peaks to D-Cys/D-CDs (Fig. S3), indicating consistency in functional group composition between L-CDs and D-CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the surface elemental composition of L-/D-CDs. Full-scan XPS survey spectra of L-/D-CDs (Fig. S5a and Fig. 1e) indicate that L-/D-CDs are composed of C, N, O and S elements. For L-CDs (D-CDs), the proportions of C, N, O and S elements are 59.86 % (56.4 %), 6.57 % (7.67 %), 29.17 % (30.53 %) and 4.4 % (5.4 %), respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. S5b and Fig. 1f) reveal the peaks at 284.7, 286.2, and 288.7 eV corresponding to C−C, C−S/C−N/C−O and C=O/C=N bonds, respectively [34–36]. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Figs. S5c and S4a) exhibit peaks at 399.9 and 401.4 eV, belonging to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [37]. The high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Figs. S5d and S4b) display peaks at 164 and 163 eV corresponding to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 in the C−S−C bond of thiophene S, with a minor peak at 168 eV indicative of sulfonate or sulfate groups (C−SOx−C, where x = 2, 3, or 4) [38,39]. Based on the above analysis, the morphological, structural, and chiral characterizations confirm that the synthesized CDs possess chirality and are composed of a carbon core with abundant surface functional groups including carboxylic acids, alcohols, or amines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of chiral CDs on the growth and cocoon formation of silkworm
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the effects of the obtained chiral CDs on silkworm was investigated, including their growth, cocoon formation, mechanical properties of cocoons and silk fibers, and the in vivo effects of CDs in silkworms. Fig. 2a illustrates the strategy to produce modified silkworms through feeding with CDs modified diets (L-/D-CDs). Initially, CDs modified diets were prepared by spraying chiral CDs solution on fresh mulberry leaves. Fifth instar silkworms were then raised by feeding with CDs modified diets, and silk fibers were obtained by degumming cocoon shells. The effect of chiral CDs on silkworms growth is illustrated in Fig. 2b and Fig. S6, showing a significant increase in body weight during the first to seventh day of the fifth instar, with growth trends comparable to the control group. In addition, all the control group, L-CDs group and D-CDs group could spin cocoons. To understand the impact of incorporating CDs on silkworm growth and cocoon formation, the morphology of silkworm cocoons and silk fibers were analyzed. Fig. 2c-f show the appearance and morphology of the cocoons of silkworm fed with CDs (1.0 mg/mL), which show no discernible differences between the cocoons from the control group and various CDs groups. Besides, all cocoons exhibit similar weights and diameters, with slightly higher values observed in CDs groups relative to the control group (Fig. 2k-l). This implies that CDs have little effect on the diameter of cocoons. The obtained cocoons in the control group and CDs groups exhibit similar colors, sizes, and weights, suggesting that the silkworms in distinct CDs groups can remain normal growth.

To examine the morphology characteristics of silk fibers, Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) was employed. Fig. 2g-j reveal no noticeable variances between the control group and CDs groups, with the surface of the degummed silk appearing smooth and cylindrical. Even CDs lead to silk fibers with smaller diameters compared with the control group (Fig. 2m), there is no significant difference between the control group and CDs groups. Feeding with CDs does not appear to exert a significant influence on the growth of silkworms and the morphology characteristics of silk fibers. Furthermore the surface characteristics of the cocoons and silk fibers from the silkworm fed by CDs with concentration of 0.5 mg/mL was also characterized, and no apparent differences observed (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties and structural characterization of silk fibers
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of chiral CDs on the mechanical properties of silk fibers was investigated. Strain-stress curves of silk fibers in the control group and L-/D-CDs groups (0.5 mg/mL and 1.0 mg/mL) are shown in Fig. S8 and Fig. 3a, respectively. In comparison to silk fibers in the control group, silk fibers in L-/D-CDs groups show higher breaking strength and elongation. The results from Fig. S8 indicate a notable increase in the breaking strength of silk fibers in L-/D-CDs groups (0.5 mg/mL) by 34.8 % (44.8 %) compared to the control group. Similarly, with the concentration of 1.0 mg/mL, the breaking strength of silk fibers in L-/D-CDs groups shows a remarkable improvement of 51.6 % (71.4 %) relative to the control group (Fig. 3a). Notably, the breaking strength and elongation can be obtained from the strain–stress curves. Silk fibers in D-CDs group display the highest breaking strength of 667.9 MPa (with an elongation at break of 23.0 %), whereas the breaking strength and elongation at break of silk fibers in the control group were 389.5 MPa and 14.8 %, respectively. These results suggest that the overall performance of silk fibers from the silkworm fed by chiral CDs is significantly enhanced. For further evaluating the mechanical properties of silk fibers, the relative stress and relative strain is defined as the increase in CDs groups relative to the control group. From Fig. 3b, the mechanical properties of silk fibers, including breaking strength and elongation at break, increase gradually with the concentration of CDs. Silk fibers in CDs groups (L-CDs, D-CDs and AC-CDs groups) exhibit excellent mechanical properties with breaking strength and elongation at break, remarkably exceeding those in the control group. Particularly, silk fibers in D-CDs group possess the best elongation and breaking strength compared to L-CDs group and AC-CDs group. These results confirm that the mechanical properties of silk fibers can be improved by the effect of CDs, with D-CDs notably reinforcing the silk fibers.

To explore the modification mechanism of CDs on the structure and properties of silk fibers, the composition and secondary structure of silk fibers were further analyzed using FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy. The secondary structure analysis of silk fibroin protein using FT-IR spectroscopy focuses on characteristic spectral regions, including the amide I band ranging from 1700 cm−1 to 1600 cm−1, the amide II band ranging from 1600 cm−1 to 1500 cm−1, and the amide III band ranging between 1350 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 [40,41]. As shown in Fig. 3c, it can be observed that all the control group and CDs groups (1.0 mg/mL) display three distinct peaks (1698 cm−1, 1656 cm−1 and 1227 cm−1). Encompassing various secondary protein structures such as β-turn structures, β-sheet structures, and α-helix structures, the amide I band is a crucial region for the analysis of silk fibroin protein. In addition, the amide I band could be divided into three characteristic peaks: β-turn structures (1660–1700 cm−1) [42], β-sheet structures (1600–1640 cm−1) [43] and a random coil/α-helix structures (1640–1660 cm−1) [44] Fig. 3d depicts that the random coil/α-helix structures content of silk fibers fed without or with CDs (1.0 mg/mL). Consistent with other modified silk fibers fed with nanoparticle, or other additives (e.g. serine) [45–47], the content of random coil/α-helix structures in CDs groups increases relative to that of the control group, indicating that CDs can prevent the transformation from random coil/α-helix structures to β-sheet structures. Particularly, the content of random coil/α-helix structures in D-CDs group significantly exceeds those of L-CDs group and AC-CDs group, revealing that the chirality of CDs would affect the secondary structure of silk fibroin protein. In addition, Fig. S9a displays the FT-IR spectra of silk fibers from the silkworms fed with CDs with concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, which display similar results as the above discussion. CDs groups exhibit higher random coil/α-helix structures content in comparison to the control group (Fig. S9b). In summary, the enhancement in a random coil/α-helix structures may be the reason for the improvement of the breaking strength and elongation at break of the modified silk fibers.

Raman spectroscopy was employed to investigate the structure changes of silk fibers as well. Figs. 3e and S10 reveal the Raman spectra of all the silk fibers in control group and CDs groups (L-CDs, D-CDs and AC-CDs groups) at the concentration of 1.0 mg/mL and 0.5 mg/mL, respectively. Three prominent peaks are observed in Raman spectroscopy. The peaks at 1085 cm−1 and 1224 cm−1 are assigned to random coil conformation and β-sheet conformation, respectively. The strong peak at 1662 cm−1 is ascribed to β-sheet/β-turn conformation [48]. These findings indicate that all the silk fibers display characteristic peaks without significant differences. Based on the results of FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy, Fig. 3f illustrates the structural changes of silk fibers between L-CDs and D-CDs. The primary structures of silk protein are β-turn structures, β-sheet structures and random coil/α-helix structures, with hydrogen bonding serving as the crucial force that sustains these configurations. The enhancement in the mechanical properties of silk fibers can be attributed to the propensity for deformable random coil/α-helix structures. Compared to L-CDs, the incorporation of D-CDs into the silk fibers may hinder the transformation of α-helix and random coils to β-sheet structures, which may further influence the properties of silk fibers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of chiral CDs on silkworm in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids play a crucial role in silk protein synthesis in silkworms, where they are transported to the silk gland following digestion and absorption. Glycine, alanine, serine, and tyrosine are the main raw materials for synthesizing silk protein. Therefore, the amino acid contents in the hemolymph of silkworms fed with different kinds of chiral CDs (1.0 mg/mL) were analyzed. The percentage of four main amino acids to the total amino acids in the silkworm hemolymph is shown in Fig. 4a. It reveals that L-CDs group and AC CDs group increase by 39.3 % and 7.9 % compared with the control group. In addition, D-CDs group exhibit the highest percentage of four main amino acids to the total amino acids in the silkworm hemolymph, increasing by 70.5 % compared with the control group, providing sufficient raw materials for the synthesis of silk proteins. Fig. 4b illustrates significant differences in the four main amino acid content in the hemolymph of silkworms fed with CDs compared with control group. In CDs treated groups, D-CDs group show the highest increase. Silkworms fed by CDs with concentration of 0.5 mg/mL show similar results in the main amino acid content of hemolymph, as shown in Fig. S11a-S11b. The feeding of CDs can promote the absorption and synthesis of the main amino acids that make up cocoon silk protein.

The midgut tissue is closely related to the digestion and absorption of nutrients. Therefore, ALP activity and GSH content in the midgut of silkworms were measured. Fig. 4c-d show the results of ALP activity and GSH content in the midgut tissue from silkworms fed with CDs (1.0 mg/mL). As shown in Fig. 4c, after feeding CDs, ALP activity is higher than that of the control group. Compared with the control group, ALP activity of L-/D-CDs groups markedly increases 65.5 % and 127.2 %, respectively. Moreover, ALP activity of silk in D-CDs group significantly exceeds that of L-CDs group and AC-CDs group. These results show that the intake of CDs could enhance the activity of ALP in silkworms, enabling larvae to digest and absorb more mulberry leaf nutrients, and strengthening the active transport process of nutrients by silkworms. In addition, GSH content of midgut in CDs groups increases obviously (Fig. 4d), 76.3 % and 89.3 % increase for L-CDs and D-CDs group, indicating that the addition of chiral CDs can enhance the antioxidant capacity of silkworms. Moreover, various CDs with the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL display similar results (Fig. S11c-S11d).

To investigate the absorption and metabolism of chiral CDs in silkworm, the confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was measured. Fig. S12a and Fig. 5a present the CLSM images of silk fibers from the silkworm fed by CDs with the concentrations of 0.5 mg/mL and 1.0 mg/mL, respectively. These images reveal that silk fibers from the silkworm fed by chiral CDs exhibit a homogeneous blue fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity values of silk fibers in the control group and CDs groups are depicted in Fig. S12c and Fig. 5c, with silk fibers in CDs group (0.5 mg/mL and 1.0 mg/mL) showing higher fluorescence than control group. In addition, fluorescence variance of silkworm excrement were explored. As shown in Fig. S12b and Fig. 5b, distinct fluorescent signals are observed in the silkworm excrement from both the control and CDs groups. Specially, the significantly higher fluorescence intensity of silkworm excrement in CDs groups (1.0 mg/mL) compared to that of the control group indicates potential metabolism of CDs (Fig. 5b). Additionally, Fig. S12d demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of excrements (silkworm fed with 0.5 mg/mL CDs) is lower than that from silkworm fed by 1.0 mg/mL CDs (Fig. 5d). These findings indicate that CDs have the capability to be metabolized and eliminated from the silk glands to the excrements.

Based on the observation above, the analysis of enzyme activity in the midgut tissue, which reflects their absorption and digestion ability, reveals that incorporating CDs can increase enzyme activity and promote the uptake of nutrients by silkworms. Furthermore, the amino acid content in the hemolymph of silkworms is similarly influenced by CDs. Following ingestion, there is a noticeable increase in the content of the main amino acids in the four types of synthetic silk protein. FT-IR spectra show that CDs increase the content of random coil/α-helix in the secondary structure of silk protein, enhancing the mechanical properties of silk fibers. The implications of these findings extend beyond the realm of silk, holding promise for the biomaterials sector. At the same time, to better utilize the characteristics of silk and achieve wider applications, it is necessary to continue to modify other properties of silk fibers.

In summary, chiral CDs were synthesized through the thermal polymerization method of CA and chiral cysteine, and the chiral biological effects of the obtained chiral CDs on silkworm were investigated. Results show that the obtained CDs do not affect the surface morphology or growth of silkworms. However, D-CDs, significantly enhance the mechanical properties of silk fibers compared with the control group and L-CDs group. In the D-CDs group with 1.0 mg/mL of CDs treatment, the breaking strength of silk fibers can increase to 667.9 MPa, showing a notable improvement of 71.4 % compared to the control group. Furthermore, silk fibers in the L-CDs group exhibit a 51.6 % increase in breaking strength. The pronounced distinction in chiral CDs could be ascribed to the alterations in the structure of silk fibroin, such as an increase in random coil/α-helix structure. Besides, the analysis of the effects of chiral CDs on silkworm in vivo displays that chiral CDs can influence the enzyme activity in the midgut and amino acid content of silkworms in the hemolymph. The percentage of four main amino acids (serine, tyrosine, glycine and alanine) to the total amino acids in the silkworm hemolymph in D-CDs group increases by 70.5 % compared to the control group, while L-CDs group increases by 39.3 %. This substantial elevation in essential amino acids in the hemolymph of silkworms treated with D-CDs offers ample raw materials required for the synthesis of silk proteins. Our work provides new ideas and approaches for studying the effects of chiral materials on living organisms.

Yan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Mengling Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Wenwen Li: Methodology. Tao Hu: Software. Yang Liu: Supervision. Hui Huang: Writing – original draft, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BE2022425, BK20230503), National Natural Science Foundation of China (52271223, 52272043, 52302107), Key R & D program of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (2022BEG02006), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.
